key_points:
## Credit Information Reporting — Directions, 2025 — Detailed Compliance Guide
*(RBI/DOR/2025-26/156, dated 2025-11-28)*

These directions require commercial banks to report credit information systematically to Credit Information Companies to enable prudent lending decisions and borrower risk assessment.

### Chapter I — Preliminary
- **Applicability:** Commercial Banks (banking companies other than Small Finance Banks, Payment Banks, and Local Area Banks; corresponding new banks; State Bank of India). **Note:** AIFIs are not directly bound; confirm applicability to NaBFID with compliance.
- **Effective date:** Immediate.
- **Credit Information Companies (CICs):** Four registered CICs: CRIF High Mark (25 Nov 2010), Equifax (26 Mar 2010), Experian (17 Feb 2010), TransUnion CIBIL (5 Mar 2012).

### Chapter II — Membership
- **Mandatory membership:** All four registered CICs.
- **One-time membership fee:** **≤ ₹10,000 per CIC**.
- **Annual membership fee:** **≤ ₹5,000 per CIC**.

### Chapter III — Credit Information Reporting
- **Data formats:** Uniform Credit Reporting Format (UCRF) (Annex I): Form 1 (Consumer), Form 2 (Commercial), Form 3 (MFI/SHG).
- **Reporting schedule:** Fortnightly, as on the **15th and last day of each month**, submitted within **7 calendar days**. Shorter intervals permitted if mutually agreed. No repayment instances (including last instalment) left unreported.
- **Consent requirement:** No longer required under CICRA; banks need not insist on obtaining consent.
- **Mandatory reporting practices:**
  - **Commercial segment:** CIN and credit history of **all directors (by DIN)**.
  - **Compromise settlements:** Report cases and reason for settlement.
  - **Part instalment overdue:** Submit as-is; qualitative filtering done by Specified Users, not the bank.
  - **"Settled" status:** Only where principal **or** interest **or** both have been waived due to financial inability; **not** for wrong debits or contested charges (report as "disputed").
  - **Commercial Papers (CP) — IPA reporting:** Issuing and Payment Agent shall report **fortnightly** (Annex II) and any default; investing banks need **not** report.
  - **Unhedged Foreign Currency Exposure (UFCE):** Report fortnightly if bank is **solo lender, consortium leader, or largest lender**.
  - **NCLT/NCLAT cases (IBC 2016):** Report as "suit-filed" cases.
  - **Relationship Segment (RS) data:** Mandatory for accounts **opened on or after 1 July 2018**; report only **Beneficial Owner shareholders** (per RBI KYC Directions, 2025).
  - **Cross-reporting:** Individual and corporate co-borrowers — consumer data in consumer format; co-borrower data in commercial format.
- **SHG member-level data (Paragraph 13):** Report all SHG member data in MFI format (Annex I, Form 3).
  - **Collection thresholds (Annex III, Tables 1–2):** Info collected where total SHG loan exceeds **₹30,000** (Table 1) or up to **₹30,000** (Table 2).
  - **Reporting threshold:** All individual SHG members under UCRF MFI format (Table 3); credit info collection **restricted to SHG members in loans exceeding ₹1,00,000**.
  - **Non-credit info:** Collect from **all SHG members, regardless of group loan amount** (Table 4 for new Savings Bank Accounts).
  - **Systems and outsourcing:** Banks must install systems/procedures; may outsource data collection **provided** RBI Outsourcing Directions, 2025 are followed and bank remains responsible for data correctness.
  - **NPA monitoring trigger:** Collect detailed info from SHG members with loans **> ₹20,000** if **Gross NPA in SHG segment > 10%** **or** **> bank's overall Gross NPA by 5 percentage points**.
  - **Non-compliance penalty:** Non-adherence excludes non-compliant SHG loan accounts from priority sector lending eligibility.
  - **Not loan pre-conditions:** Data requirements **shall not** be made pre-conditions for lending; sincere compliance efforts required.
  - **Credit decisions on defaulting SHGs/members:** Develop policies; do **not** deny loans solely due to defaults; evaluate individual credit history and economic viability.

### Chapter IV — Post-Cancellation and Data Quality
- **Licence cancellation (Paragraph 14):** Cancelled-licence banks categorized as "Credit Institution" under CICRA § 2(f)(vii); **shall continue reporting** to all four CICs until loan lifecycle completion or wind-up (whichever earlier); **shall have access only to borrowers on-boarded pre-cancellation**; **exempt from annual and membership fees**; applies retroactively to pre-2025 cancellations.
- **Rejected data rectification (Paragraph 15):** Rectify and re-submit within **7 days** of rejection notice.
- **Half-yearly DQI review (Paragraph 17):** Half-yearly review of Data Quality Index; report issues and corrective steps to **top management within 2 months** of half-year end.
- **Correction at source (Paragraph 19):** Maintain arrangements to receive customer rectification requests; correct erroneous data at source.
- **SARFAESI possession display (Paragraph 21):** Banks that are secured creditors **shall display on website** info on borrowers' secured assets taken into possession under SARFAESI Act, 2002 (Annex IV format).
implications:
# Why NaBFID Should Care About the Commercial Banks Credit Information Reporting Direction

**Benchmark and Regulatory Reference:**
Although this direction is formally addressed to commercial banks, NaBFID must care about it for two reasons: (1) it sets the benchmark standard that RBI's separate AIFI directions are typically benchmarked against or compared to, helping NaBFID understand where its own obligations may be stricter, looser, or aligned; and (2) RBI's AIFI directions frequently incorporate provisions from the Commercial Banks directions by cross-reference, meaning parts of this direction may directly bind NaBFID depending on what the AIFI credit information reporting direction says.

**Why It Matters for Infrastructure Lending:**
Credit information reporting ensures that default histories, restructuring events, and loan characteristics flow to credit information companies (CICs). For a long-tenor infrastructure lender like NaBFID, this affects how project defaults or covenant waivers are reported—influencing future borrower creditworthiness and the ecosystem's risk assessment of infrastructure sponsors.

**Illustrative Example:**
If NaBFID restructures a highway project loan to extend tenor by two years without a formal default, the Commercial Banks direction may require the bank to report this as a "restructured advance" to CICs. NaBFID must verify whether its own AIFI direction cross-refers to this reporting standard or sets a different one—a compliance team check is essential before treating infrastructure restructurings as off-record.
